schools.summary: Ellicott reads the opening formula as a mere mark of transition and takes Chronicles' 'Gath and her towns' as the chronicler's interpretation of Samuel's obscure 'bridle of the mother-city'; Keil and Delitzsch cite the same paraphrase as proof the chronicler drew independently on the older source, not on Samuel.

Matthew Henry: after his communion with God in the previous chapter David goes on conquering; the long-vexatious Philistines are subdued, a pledge that all opposing powers shall be put down by the Son of David.
Clarke lays out the whole campaign of the chapter and refers throughout to the parallel in 2 Samuel 8.

themes: enemies of Israel subdued at last

schools.summary: Both read the Moab notice as much abridged from Samuel; the omission of David's harsh treatment of the prisoners is not apologetic bias (he records the like at Rabbah, 1 Chr 20:3) but the chronicler's method. Ellicott fixes the idiom of 'became servants' and 'brought gifts' (tribute) and points to the Moabite Stone.

Ellicott: Chronicles' 'Hadarezer' is a slip for Samuel's correct 'Hadadezer' ('Hadad is help'), the Syrian god's name buried in the king's; he sets Zobah near Emesa, connects the war with the coalition of 1 Chronicles 19, and parses 'stablish his dominion' as 'set up his hand.'
Matthew Henry: such is the world's uncertainty that men lose their power when they think to confirm it — Hadadezer smitten as he went to establish his dominion.

themes: a god's name in a king's

schools.summary: Ellicott weighs the variant numbers against Samuel (favouring Chronicles' fuller reading), explains 'houghed' as hamstrung, and notes David reserved a hundred chariots — horses being a luxury in Israel, restrained by the law of Deuteronomy 17:16.
Matthew Henry: David houghed the chariot-horses because he believed 'a horse is a vain thing for safety' (Ps. 33:17); resolved by Ps. 20:7 not to trust them, he would not use them.

themes: a horse a vain thing for safety

schools.summary: Ellicott renders 'Aram of Damascus came,' traces the city's name across Syriac, Arabic, and cuneiform forms, and reads 'smote in Aram' as partitive — a great part struck, not the whole nation annihilated.
Matthew Henry: the enemies of God's church ruin themselves by helping one another — Damascus is smitten because it came to succour Hadadezer, gathered like sheaves to the floor (Micah 4:11-12).

themes: the futility of leaguing against the anointed

schools.summary: Ellicott supplies the dropped noun 'garrisons' (governors, cf. 1 Kings 4:19), notes 'preserved' means 'made victorious' in a late construction, and reads the 'shields of gold' as arms or armour generally; Damascus becomes a vassal, its king not dethroned.
Matthew Henry seizes the refrain 'the LORD preserved David whithersoever he went' — those are always under God's eye who have God always in their eye.
Ellicott has only the briefest note, that Chronicles' 'on the servants' answers to Samuel's 'to,' i.e. arms belonging to Hadadezer's officers.

Matthew Henry: the wealth of the sinner is laid up for the just — the golden shields go up to Jerusalem; as the tabernacle was of the spoils of Egypt, so the temple of the spoils of the Gentiles, a presage of the Gentiles' interest in the gospel church.

themes: the wealth of the sinner for the just

schools.summary: Ellicott untangles the place-names (Tibhath/Betah, Chun/Berothai as textual variants of real Syrian towns), renders 'brass' as copper or bronze, and marks the added clause that Solomon made the temple furnishings from this metal.
Matthew Henry: the brass, like the gold, was dedicated toward the building of the temple — the spoils of the Gentiles becoming, as with the tabernacle and Egypt, the furniture of worship.

themes: the brass of Tibhath and Chun
spoil traced to the temple vessels
the sword's plunder given to God

schools.summary: Ellicott settles 'Tou' (Samuel's 'Toi,' w/y confusion), records the versions' errors ('Phul king of the Antiochenes'), and cites Sayce's reading of the name on the Hamath stones.
Clarke notes this Tou is the Toi of 2 Samuel 8:9.

Matthew Henry: it is our interest to befriend those who have God's presence; the king of Hamath courts David — 'Kiss the Son, lest he be angry' — but the gifts God seeks are our hearts, our whole selves as living sacrifices.

Ellicott weighs 'Hadoram' against Samuel's 'Joram'/Jehoram, noting Sargon's evidence that the God of Israel was known among the Hamathites; 'congratulate' is literally 'bless,' and Hadadezer had been 'a man of wars' of Tou.
Matthew Henry (reading vv.9-10 together): a rival delivered from the common enemy hastens to secure the victor's friendship — the homage of a Gentile king unbidden and unbeaten.

themes: a possible whisper of the LORD in Aram

schools.summary: Ellicott notes the chronicler's 'brought' for Samuel's 'dedicated,' corrects 'from Edom' (not 'Aram'), refers 'Amalek' to Ziklag or an unrecorded war, and observes Chronicles omits the Hadadezer clause as already implied.

Matthew Henry: what God blesses us with we must honour him with — David dedicated friends' gifts and enemies' spoils alike to the LORD; that is truly our own which is consecrated to God ('holiness to the LORD,' Isaiah 23:18).
Clarke reconciles the attribution — Chronicles credits Abishai, Samuel David — by the maxim 'qui facit per alterum, facit per se': David as commander-in-chief owns the victory his captain won.

Ellicott collates the three passages (reading Samuel's 'Aram' as 'Edom'), reconstructs Edom's invasion of Judah during David's northern absence, and identifies the Valley of Salt with the battlefield of 2 Kings 14:7.

themes: a kingdom victorious on every side

schools.summary: Ellicott reads the garrisons as the complete subjugation of Edom and surveys David victorious on every side; the six peoples he reduced had been the foes of Saul (1 Samuel 14:47-48), harassed by Saul but subdued by David.
Matthew Henry: the refrain 'the LORD preserved David whithersoever he went,' repeated from v.6, discloses the true cause of his success — the presence of God, not the strength of his garrisons.

themes: the presence of God the secret of success

schools.summary: Ellicott parses 'executed' as 'was doing' (a permanent state), 'judgment and justice' as the quality and the conduct that embodies it, and 'among' as 'unto' — a constant, universal, active righteousness.

Matthew Henry: God gives power to do good, not to look great; David, not so intent on conquest abroad as to neglect justice at home, becomes a type of the Messiah, the sceptre of whose kingdom is a right sceptre.

themes: David a type of the righteous Messiah

schools.summary: Clarke names the offices: Joab general-in-chief 'over the host,' and Jehoshaphat the 'recorder,' the king's remembrancer or historiographer royal.

Ellicott expounds 'recorder' as 'remembrancer' — keeper of the memoranda and acts of state, a weighty office recurring among Judah's great officers (cf. 2 Chronicles 34:8), closer to a chancellor than a scribe.
Clarke: Zadok and Abimelech both high priests, one at Gibeon and one at Jerusalem (cf. 1 Chronicles 16:39); Shavsha the scribe is the Seraiah of 2 Samuel 8:17.

Ellicott traces Zadok to Eleazar, reads 'Abimelech' as 'Ahimelech son of Abiathar,' weighs and declines the proposed emendation to 'Abiathar son of Ahimelech' (an Ahimelech son of Abiathar being priest as deputy, 1 Chr 24:3), and notes Shavsha/Sheva/Seraiah.

debates.question: Should 'Abimelech the son of Abiathar' be emended to 'Abiathar the son of Ahimelech'?

sides.position: Yes — invert the names to match the known priesthood of Abiathar son of the Ahimelech slain at Nob
No — let the received text stand; an Ahimelech son of Abiathar served as deputy, and it is arbitrary to alter four passages

schools.summary: Ellicott identifies the Cherethites and Pelethites as the foreign (Philistine) bodyguard under Benaiah and explains 'the first at the king's hand' as the chronicler's softening of Samuel's 'priests' (kohanim), lest David's sons seem to hold a sacred office; Keil and Delitzsch mark this as one of the chronicler's characteristic paraphrases.
Clarke adds the Targum's gloss making Benaiah president of the Sanhedrin and consulter of Urim and Thummim, and notes the sons of David were highest in authority.

themes: David's sons chief about the king
